label: Indigenous Population (Pre-Contact c.1491)
source: William Denevan (1992) / Koch et al., The Lancet (2019)
contestNote: Estimates range from 8 million (Kroeber, 1939) to 145 million (Dobyns, 1966); scholarly consensus converges around 54–65 million for the Americas.

label: Indigenous Population Loss (1492–1600)
source: Koch et al., The Lancet: Planetary Health (2019)
contestNote: Primary cause is debated: epidemic disease (Crosby), deliberate violence and overwork (Stannard), or a combination. Most scholars accept 80–95% decline in first century.

label: Silver Mined at Potosí (1545–1800)
source: Archivo General de Indias / Kris Lane, Potosí (2019)
contestNote: Represents approximately 60% of all silver produced in the world from 1500–1800, fundamentally reshaping global trade.

label: Africans Enslaved and Transported (1500–1900)
source: Trans-Atlantic Slave Trade Database (slavevoyages.org, Eltis et al.)
contestNote: Based on documented voyage records; approximately 1.8 million died during the Middle Passage crossing.

label: Duration of Conquest Era
contestNote: From Columbus's first landing (October 12, 1492) to the Treaty of Paris ending Spanish empire (December 10, 1898).

label: Estimated Indigenous Languages at Contact
contestNote: Hundreds of languages became extinct in the first two centuries of conquest; approximately 500–700 remain today in the Americas.

label: Cortés's Force at Tenochtitlan (with allies)
source: Ross Hassig, Mexico and the Spanish Conquest (1994)
contestNote: Only ~500 were Spanish; the vast majority were indigenous allies, particularly Tlaxcalans. The conquest was fundamentally an indigenous civil war enabled by Spanish technology and disease.

label: Atahualpa's Ransom (1532–33)
source: Francisco de Jerez, Verdadera relación (1534) / John Hemming (1970)
contestNote: The largest ransom in recorded history. Pizarro executed Atahualpa anyway, violating the agreement.

label: Deaths in Potosí Mita Labor System (est.)
source: Eduardo Galeano, Open Veins of Latin America / Bartolomé Arzáns de Orsúa (1736)
contestNote: Total figure over 250+ years is disputed; conservative estimates range from 1–8 million deaths. Conditions are not disputed — only the cumulative death toll.
